agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle-class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This spans multiple bounded contexts: Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Lifecycle, and Customer Notification.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, zone assignments, shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-progress, failed, completed), zone assignment of parcels; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery proximity windows, customer communication preferences. The feature described crosses all four contexts, making integration seams critical.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out or exhaust assigned deliveries. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic region used to partition deliveries and assign drivers. A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift. Deliveries belong to exactly one zone." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their zone during a shift." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but not yet assigned to a driver's route or attempted." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that did not result in successful handoff to the recipient. Triggers route recalculation to remove this stop and re-optimize remaining stops." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation", "definition": "The act of re-optimizing the remaining stops on a driver's active route, triggered by specific domain events (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation)." },
      { "term": "Manual Deviation", "definition": "When a driver physically departs from the suggested route sequence — detected by the system (e.g., GPS) — indicating the current optimization is no longer valid." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity threshold defined as the 3 upcoming stops in route order. When a delivery enters this window, the associated customer is notified." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of vehicle type (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight) that constrains which deliveries a driver can be assigned. Determined by the driver's vehicle for the shift." },
      { "term": "Delivery Vehicle Class Requirement", "definition": "A property of a delivery/parcel that specifies the minimum vehicle class needed to transport it (based on size, weight, hazmat, refrigeration, etc.)." },
      { "term": "Stop Time Estimate", "definition": "The assumed duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Operations Team", "definition": "Internal stakeholders who configure operational parameters like zone-level stop time overrides. They do not own routes but influence optimization inputs." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system MUST automatically generate an optimized route from all pending deliveries in the driver's assigned zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy — the business could change to manual assignment or batch-based assignment. The trigger (shift start) is a business decision, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver starts a shift in zone Z, the system produces a route containing all pending deliveries in zone Z, ordered by optimization algorithm output."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery whose vehicle class requirement exceeds the driver's licensed vehicle class for their current shift.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight. Assigning such a delivery would be unsafe, illegal, and undeliverable. If this invariant is violated, the system state is corrupt — it has promised something physically impossible.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery D on driver X's route: D.requiredVehicleClass is compatible with X.currentVehicleClass. Any attempt to assign an incompatible delivery MUST be rejected."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's active zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an optimization policy. The business could decide to batch new deliveries and recalculate every 15 minutes instead. The system is not corrupt if a new delivery briefly exists without being on a route.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given driver X has an active route in zone Z, when a new delivery is created in zone Z, then the system triggers a route recalculation that includes the new delivery in the optimized sequence."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the active route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy — the system could technically continue with the original route minus the failed stop. Recalculation is a business preference for efficiency, not a data integrity requirement.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given driver X has an active route, when delivery D on that route is marked failed, then the system recalculates the route excluding D and re-optimizing the remaining stops."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Optimization policy. The driver could continue with the old route from their new position. Recalculation is a UX and efficiency decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given driver X deviates from the suggested next stop, the system detects the deviation and triggers a route recalculation from the driver's current position."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Notification policy — the window size (3 stops) is a business preference. A VP could change it to 5 stops or switch to time-based proximity. Not a data integrity issue.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a route is calculated or recalculated, for each delivery now in positions 1-3 of the route that was NOT previously in the next-3 window, a customer notification is dispatched."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated time per delivery stop is 4 minutes. This default CAN be overridden by the operations team on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Configurable operational parameter. Both the default value and the override mechanism are business policy decisions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Route time calculations use the zone-specific override if one exists; otherwise, the 4-minute default. The operations team can set, update, and remove zone overrides."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A route recalculation MUST only re-optimize REMAINING (undelivered, non-failed) stops. Completed stops are never reintroduced.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "State integrity — a completed delivery cannot be 'un-completed.' Reintroducing it would corrupt delivery state and potentially cause duplicate delivery attempts, which has real-world financial and customer impact.",
      "testable_assertion": "After recalculation, the new route contains zero deliveries with status 'completed' or 'failed'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A driver can have at most ONE active route at a time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A driver is a single physical person who can only be in one place. Two active routes simultaneously would be a corrupt system state — conflicting instructions, double-counted ETAs, duplicate notifications.",
      "testable_assertion": "At any point in time, querying active routes for driver X returns exactly 0 or 1 results."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "A delivery can exist on at most ONE active route at a time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A parcel is a physical object in one location. Assigning it to two drivers simultaneously would result in wasted trips and conflicting customer notifications. This is a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any delivery D with status 'assigned', exactly one active route contains D."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and is assigned to zone Z, and there are 12 pending deliveries in zone Z compatible with the driver's vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route is generated containing exactly those 12 compatible deliveries, ordered by the optimization algorithm, and the driver receives the route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver with a motorcycle vehicle class starts a shift in zone Z, and zone Z has 10 pending deliveries (8 standard parcels, 2 freight shipments)",
      "when": "The route is generated",
      "then": "The route contains only the 8 standard parcels. The 2 freight shipments are excluded and remain in the pending pool for a compatible driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 7 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new delivery is added to the driver's zone and is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, re-optimizing the order of now 8 remaining stops from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with stops [A, B, C, D, E] and delivery B is marked as failed",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The route is recalculated with remaining deliveries [A, C, D, E] (excluding B), re-optimized from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver's route has next stops [A, B, C, D, E] and the driver skips A and drives toward D",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position with all remaining undelivered stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A route is calculated or recalculated, and delivery X is now in position 2 (within the next-3 window) and was previously in position 5",
      "when": "The route update is committed",
      "then": "Customer for delivery X receives a notification that their delivery is arriving soon. Customers for deliveries already notified in a previous window are NOT re-notified"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone Z has a stop time override of 6 minutes, and zone Y uses the default",
      "when": "Routes are calculated for drivers in both zones",
      "then": "Zone Z route uses 6 minutes per stop for ETA calculations; zone Y route uses 4 minutes per stop"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver has completed 3 of 10 deliveries and a recalculation is triggered",
      "when": "The new route is generated",
      "then": "The 3 completed deliveries do NOT appear on the new route. Only the 7 remaining deliveries (minus any failed) are included"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "Two drivers are active in zone Z simultaneously",
      "when": "Routes are generated",
      "then": "No delivery appears on both drivers' routes simultaneously. Pending deliveries are partitioned, not duplicated"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A new delivery is added to a zone but no active driver has a compatible vehicle class", "expected_behavior": "The delivery remains in the pending pool. It is NOT force-assigned to an incompatible driver. The operations team should be alerted that unserviceable deliveries exist in the zone." },
    { "scenario": "A route recalculation is triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The system must handle concurrent recalculation requests gracefully — either queue them and process the latest state, or cancel the in-flight calculation and start fresh. The driver must never receive a stale or partial route." },
    { "scenario": "A driver starts a shift and there are zero pending deliveries in their zone", "expected_behavior": "The driver is assigned an empty route (or placed in an idle/waiting state). When a delivery arrives in their zone, recalculation triggers and they receive their first stop." },
    { "scenario": "All remaining deliveries on a route are marked as failed", "expected_behavior": "The route becomes empty. The driver is placed in an idle state or their shift is flagged for operations review. No customer notifications are sent." },
    { "scenario": "A customer's delivery repeatedly enters and exits the next-3 window due to frequent recalculations (e.g., many new deliveries being added)", "expected_behavior": "The customer should receive at most ONE 'arriving soon' notification per delivery attempt. Notification deduplication MUST be enforced to prevent notification spam." },
    { "scenario": "A driver deviates from the route but their deviation happens to align with a later stop (they skip ahead)", "expected_behavior": "The system still recalculates from the driver's current position. If the driver is near stop 5 (having skipped 2-4), the recalculation should recognize stops 2-4 as still pending and re-incorporate them." },
    { "scenario": "The operations team changes the zone stop time override while drivers have active routes", "expected_behavior": "Active routes should recalculate ETAs using the new stop time on the next recalculation event. The system should NOT retroactively change ETAs for already-sent customer notifications." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added to a zone but its vehicle class requirement is ambiguous or missing", "expected_behavior": "The system should default to the most restrictive interpretation (require largest vehicle class) or reject the delivery as unroutable until classified. Must not silently assign to a motorcycle driver." },
    { "scenario": "A driver's shift ends while they still have remaining deliveries", "expected_behavior": "Remaining undelivered stops return to the pending pool and become available for reassignment to another driver or the next shift." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (new delivery added AND a delivery fails at the same instant)", "expected_behavior": "The system should coalesce these into a single recalculation that accounts for both events, not two sequential recalculations." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs and increases deliveries per shift (driver utilization). Vehicle class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts (wasted trip + re-delivery cost). Accurate ETAs from correct stop time estimates reduce missed deliveries and customer callbacks. Poor implementation risks: wasted fuel from suboptimal routes, penalty costs from SLA breaches, labor cost overruns from overtime caused by bad routing.",
    "operational": "Automatic route generation eliminates manual dispatch work. Dynamic recalculation maintains route quality throughout the shift without dispatcher intervention. However, frequent recalculations could overwhelm the optimization engine — throttling/coalescing is operationally critical. The operations team gains a new responsibility: managing per-zone stop time overrides, requiring a configuration UI and audit trail.",
    "user": "Drivers get clear, optimized instructions at shift start and dynamic updates during their shift, reducing cognitive load and decision fatigue. Customers get proactive 'arriving soon' notifications improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my package' inquiries. Risk of notification spam from frequent recalculations could degrade customer experience if deduplication is not implemented.",
    "risk": "HIGH: Vehicle class enforcement failure could result in safety incidents (overloaded motorcycles) or regulatory violations. MEDIUM: Route recalculation storms during peak hours could cause system performance degradation. MEDIUM: Deviation detection false positives (GPS drift, tunnel) could trigger unnecessary recalculations and confuse drivers. LOW: Stop time estimate inaccuracy compounds across many stops, causing late-shift ETA drift.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary users. Receive optimized routes automatically. Experience changes with every recalculation. Must trust the system's route quality or they will ignore it and deviate more — creating a feedback loop of recalculations." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive 'arriving soon' notifications. Better visibility into delivery timing. Risk of notification spam or inaccurate ETAs if stop times are misconfigured." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "New configuration responsibility (zone stop times). Reduced manual dispatch workload. Need visibility into unassigned deliveries (vehicle class mismatches) and route health." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Role may be significantly reduced or changed. Previously assigned routes manually — now the system automates this. Need to monitor edge cases (empty zones, all-failed routes)." },
      { "group": "Finance/Business", "impact": "Expects measurable improvement in deliveries-per-shift and fuel cost reduction. Needs KPIs: average route efficiency, recalculation frequency, delivery success rate." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 62,
    "reasoning": "The spec covers the happy path well and names explicit triggers for recalculation. However, several critical domain details are unspecified: How are deliveries partitioned between multiple drivers in the same zone? What defines 'manual deviation' precisely (GPS threshold? skipping a stop?)? What is the vehicle class hierarchy and compatibility matrix? What happens to in-flight routes when shifts end? The notification deduplication strategy is unmentioned. These gaps create real implementation risk.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class enforcement is a hard invariant — spec is clear and unambiguous",
      "Recalculation triggers are explicitly enumerated (3 triggers)",
      "Stop time default (4 min) with per-zone override is clearly specified",
      "Customer notification threshold (next 3 stops) is explicitly stated"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver delivery partitioning within the same zone — no allocation strategy specified",
      "Definition of 'manual deviation' — GPS drift tolerance, detection mechanism unspecified",
      "Vehicle class hierarchy and compatibility rules — only motorcycle/freight example given",
      "Shift lifecycle boundaries — what happens to routes when shifts end or overlap",
      "Notification deduplication — not mentioned but critical for customer experience",
      "Recalculation throttling — no mention of how to handle rapid successive triggers"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift (spec says 'their zone' implying singular)",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a strict hierarchy, not a tag-based system",
      "Route optimization considers travel distance/time, not other factors like delivery priority or time windows",
      "The 'next 3 stops' notification is based on route position, not geographic proximity or time",
      "Failed deliveries are permanently removed from the route (not retried within the same shift)",
      "A delivery's zone assignment is immutable once created"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "How are pending deliveries partitioned between multiple active drivers in the same zone? The spec says 'all pending deliveries in their zone' but if two drivers start shifts in zone Z simultaneously, they cannot both get all pending deliveries. Is it first-come-first-served? Split by geography within the zone? By vehicle class? By load capacity?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: First driver gets all compatible deliveries; second driver gets only new arrivals",
        "B: Deliveries are geographically partitioned within the zone (sub-zones)",
        "C: Optimization algorithm jointly optimizes across all active drivers in the zone",
        "D: Operations team manually partitions, system optimizes within each partition"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C (joint optimization) is the most domain-correct approach in logistics, but Option D is simpler and gives dispatchers control. Need product team input."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What precisely constitutes a 'manual deviation'? Is it GPS-based (driver is X meters from expected path)? Is it action-based (driver marks 'skip stop')? What is the tolerance threshold to avoid false positives from GPS drift, tunnels, or traffic detours?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: GPS deviation beyond X meters from route for Y seconds",
        "B: Driver arrives at a stop that is not the next expected stop",
        "C: Driver explicitly signals deviation via app",
        "D: Combination of GPS and stop-sequence detection"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option D — GPS alone is unreliable. Recommend detecting deviation when the driver completes a stop out of sequence OR when GPS shows sustained deviation beyond a configurable threshold. Need product team to define thresholds."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The vehicle class hierarchy and compatibility matrix is undefined. The spec gives one example (motorcycle cannot carry freight) but does not define the full set of vehicle classes, their capabilities, or the compatibility rules. Is a van compatible with 'car' deliveries? Can a truck handle motorcycle-class deliveries?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define a strict linear hierarchy (motorcycle < car < van < truck < freight) where higher can handle lower",
        "B: Define a capability-based system (weight limit, volume limit, refrigeration, hazmat) per vehicle class",
        "C: Simple tag matching — delivery requires exactly the tagged vehicle class"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B is the most domain-accurate for logistics (a van has both weight and volume limits, some need refrigeration). Option A is acceptable as an MVP if deliveries only vary by size. Need domain expert input on actual vehicle classes in use."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not address delivery time windows or priority levels. In real logistics, some deliveries have promised delivery windows (e.g., 'before 2 PM') or priority tiers (express vs standard). Should the route optimization honor these constraints, or is pure distance/time optimization sufficient?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations Team", "Finance/Business"],
      "options": [
        "A: Pure distance/time optimization — no time windows considered",
        "B: Time-window-aware optimization — hard constraint that must be met",
        "C: Priority-weighted optimization — express deliveries are weighted higher but no hard time windows"
      ],
      "recommendation": "This is a critical business question. If the platform has SLAs with time windows, Option B is mandatory. If not, Option A is acceptable for MVP. Need product team confirmation."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
